title: Burning Bridges and Building New Ones Burning Bridges Accept at the most radical level that you are not going to engage in addictive behavior again, and then move actively to cut off all addictive behavior options.

pages.text:
362
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
Distress Tolerance Handout 20 (Distress Tolerance Worksheet 17)
Burning Bridges and Building New Ones
Burning Bridges
Accept at the most radical level that you are not going to engage in addictive behavior
again, and then move actively to cut off all addictive behavior options.

 1.	Make an absolute commitment to abstinence from the addictive behavior, which is (describe addictive behavior). Then walk into the garage of
abstinence and slam the garage door shut. (Remember that the tiniest slit of space
can let an entire elephant in.)

 2.	List everything in your life that makes addiction possible.

 3.	Get rid of these things:

 Throw out contact information of people who collude with you.

 Get rid of all possible cues and temptations.

 4.	List and do everything you can that will make it hard or impossible to continue your
addictive behavior.

 Ruthlessly and at every moment, tell the truth about your behavior.

 Tell all your friends and family that you have quit.
Building New Bridges
Create visual images and smells that will compete with the information loaded into
your visual and olfactory brain systems when cravings occur.
Cravings and urges are strongly related to vivid images and smells of what is craved. The stron-
ger the imagery or smell, the stronger the craving.

 Build different images or smells to think about. Try to keep these images or smells
in memory when you have an unwanted craving. For example, whenever you crave
a cigarette, imagine being on the beach; see and smell it in your mind to reduce the
craving.

 When you have unwanted cravings, look at moving images or surround yourself with
smells unrelated to the addiction. Moving images and new smells will compete with your
cravings.

 “Urge-surf” by imagining yourself on a surfboard riding the waves of your urges. Notice
them coming and going, rising high, going low, and finally going away.

copyright_notice: Extracted from user-provided PDF: DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Respect source copyright and purchaser-use limits.

ourspace_kindness_pass.title: Burning Bridges and Building New Ones Burning Bridges Accept at the most radical level that you are not going to engage in addictive behavior again, and then move actively to cut off all addictive behavior options.

main_doability_needs: bed-level or seated options by default
oxygen/breathing, pain, vertigo, fatigue, mobility, and recovery time respected
ADHD/autism supports: transitions, low-stimulation starts, tiny visible steps
partial completion and stopping before a crash count as success
no shame, no body blame, no pressure to push through pain or dysphoria
ADHD transition support before task demands
caregiver burnout recovery treated as care, not avoidance
protected rest, boundaries, decompression, sensory needs, and people-pleasing checks
no assumption that Jasper has unlimited caregiver capacity

household_rules: no hour block may hold more than two tasks/tools
skills may be used as one-breath or one-field versions
survival days load critical care first and skip optional worksheets
rest, boundaries, decompression, and asking for help count as care
skills are for support, not grades

universal_kindness_rules: This is a support tool, not a test.
One line, one checkbox, one breath, or one tiny step counts.
Skipping a skill because the body is unsafe, exhausted, dysregulated, or overloaded is allowed.
Do not stack this with more than one other task inside the same hour.
Survival-day version always wins over perfection.
william_accessibility_adaptations: Use bed-level, seated, voice-note, or caregiver-assisted versions when needed.
Avoid pain escalation, respiratory strain, unsafe transfers, and dysphoria-triggering language.
Break worksheets into one-field chunks; stopping before a crash is success.
jasper_accessibility_adaptations: Start with transition support before expecting focus.
Protect caregiver burnout recovery; rest and boundaries are valid completion.
Use body-doubling, timers, low-stimulation versions, and one-minute starts.
tiny_steps: Choose one sentence or tool that fits today.
Use the low-spoon version; no full worksheet is required.

routine_integration.routine_role: monthly review or larger pattern tool
routine_integration.start_the_day_use: not auto-loaded on survival days; use as a scheduled monthly review item
routine_integration.recommended_block: monthly support audit or household reset
